retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis is an immediate hypersensitivity reaction (type 1) that can also occur when using mAbs. Anaphylaxis occurs due to the development of IgE antibodies against mAbs and is generally not expected to occur on the first exposure of the recipient to any given mAb. This is because sensitization to the mAb cannot occur without prior exposure, but cases of host IgE cross-reactivity have been documented. Anaphylaxis is classified as a type beta reaction per the classification scheme proposed by Pichler et al. (2006). [7] [8] The management of mAb-associated anaphylaxis does not differ from the management of anaphylaxis due to other agents.

retrieved_docs.title: Type II Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should be aware of the potential for medications such as fluoroquinolones, macrolides, and neuromuscular blocking agents to exacerbate myasthenia gravis. Pyridostigmine improves neuromuscular transmission and is first-line in patients with mild symptoms of myasthenia gravis. Biologics such as rozanolixizumab, IVIG, glucocorticoids, or plasma exchange are used in patients with refractory symptoms or myasthenic crisis. Thymectomy is beneficial in early-onset or thymoma-associated disease. Fc receptor antagonists, such as efgartigimod, and C5 inhibitors, such as eculizumab, represent novel targeted therapies for severe cases. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Adverse drug reactions (ADRs) are classically classified into five subcategories (A-E) in pharmacology. Type A reactions (augmented) are expected, dose-dependent reactions, and due to the pharmacology of the respective agent. Type B (bizarre) reactions are not related to the pharmacology of the respective medication and, thus, are not predictable. They are not dose-dependent and may range from immune-mediated ADRs to hypersensitivity reactions. Type C (chronic) reactions are dependant on drug metabolism and are continuous. Type D (delayed) reactions are typically challenging to identify from a diagnostic perspective and encompass ADRs that can occur with prolonged (years) use of medications. Type E (end of treatment) reactions occur after withdrawal of medication from a treatment regimen. This classification scheme works well to encompass most drugs that are chemical compounds (xenobiotics) but may prove insufficient to classify mAb associated ADRs. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonus -- Treatment / Management -- Precision Medicine
retrieved_docs.content: Though still in its infancy as a therapeutic approach to myoclonus, precision therapies are targets of mounting interest for pharmacotherapy. In neuroinflammatory disorders, for example, "designer" monoclonal antibodies that target chemo- and cytokines along with soluble receptor ligands and proteomic small molecule therapies represent recent advances with exciting potential for targeted therapies. [74]

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine With Aura -- Treatment / Management -- Calcitonin gene-related peptide antagonists
retrieved_docs.content: CGRP antagonists are reasonable options for patients who are unresponsive to the above options. They are available in parenteral, large molecules monoclonal antibodies targeting the CGRP receptor and oral small-molecule antagonists. Eptinezumab dosed 100 to 300 mg IV infusion once every 3 months Fremanezumab dosed 225 mg subcutaneously once monthly or 675 mg once every 3 months Atogepant 10, 30, or 60 mg orally once daily Rimegepant 75 mg orally every other day for prevention and 75 mg once daily for acute treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: As outlined above, mAbs are proteins that have been engineered to closely reflect the structure of naturally produced human proteins, and thus, are similarly handled by the body (i.e., not metabolized like drugs). A review article by Pichler et al. (2006) published a classification system to better classify the ADRs associated with the use of mAbs. [7] [8] Type alpha reactions refer to high cytokine and cytokine release syndrome reactions. These reactions occur due to the administration or release of high doses of cytokines into the systemic circulation. Type beta reactions are allergy-type reactions that may be mediated by IgE, IgG, or T-cells (immediate vs. delayed). Type gamma reactions are termed cytokine or immune imbalance syndromes that can lead to autoimmunity, allergic/atopic disorders, or impaired immune function. Type delta reactions refer to cross-reactivity reactions that can occur when targeted antigens are also expressed on other cells/ structures. Type epsilon reactions refer to non-immunologic adverse effects. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Epilepsia Partialis Continua -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The underlying pathological condition leading to epilepsia partialis continua (EPC) is the initial focus of the management strategy. The central aspect of any treatment regimen is seizure control using anti-epileptic medication(AED). Some drugs have been seen to be more effective than others, but polytherapy using multiple AEDs is fairly common. Studies have shown that phenytoin and phenobarbital may be more effective than valproate or carbamazepine. Newer AEDs like levetiracetam, brivaracetam, lacosamide, and zonisamide may also be used as they are well tolerated. [19] [20] Therapy with felbamate has also shown positive results, particularly in resistant cases. [21] Rasmussen encephalitis has been seen to be responsive to immunosuppressive agents such as rituximab, tacrolimus, intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG), and plasma exchange. [22] [23] [24] Evidence has also shown some efficacy of ganciclovir and intraventricular interferon alfa in extremely small cohorts. [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Omalizumab is a recombinant humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody that specifically targets human immunoglobulin E (IgE). This action prevents the drug's interaction with the high-affinity Fc-epsilon-RI receptor, typically found on eosinophils, mast cells, and basophils. This interaction plays a critical role in the allergic cascade. [1] Omalizumab is vital in managing moderate-to-severe asthma mediated by IgE and, more recently, treating chronic urticaria.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Angioedema -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment is unique from angioedema associated with histamine since antihistamines, corticosteroids, and epinephrine have little effect on the swelling. Instead, medications that either inhibitor production of bradykinin or compete with the receptor are utilized. [7] [8] Most experts divide therapy into 3 types: treatment of attacks, short-term prevention of attacks, and long-term prevention of attacks. The drugs used for treating attacks include ecallantide, icatibant, C1-inhibitor derived from human plasma, or recombinant C1-inhibitor. In addition, fresh frozen plasma can be used but is avoided due to allosensitization unless one of the previous drugs is not available. Because of the short half-life of icatibant and ecallantide, neither are effective in preventing attacks. C1-inhibitor obviously works by replacing the dysfunctional or deficit C1-inhibitor, and it has a long half-life and can be used for the treatment of attacks, short-term prophylaxis, such as before a procedure, and long-term prophylaxis to prevent attacks when indicated. Similarly, recent data demonstrate that recombinant C1-inhibitor can be used for attacks and long-term prophylaxis, and most experts suspect it is also effective for short-term prophylaxis. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Emicizumab -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluating And Referring Patients For Outpatient Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus In The Emergency Department (Archived) -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Although generally well-tolerated, administration of monoclonal antibodies is associated with the risk of immune-mediated reactions, including anaphylaxis, serum sickness, and antibody generation. Besides these immune-mediated reactions, the adverse effects of monoclonal antibodies are also related to their specific targets and are largely unknown due to the limited availability of published literature. Safety data from clinical trials evaluating monoclonal antibodies reported are summarized below. The most frequently reported adverse effects of sotrovimab therapy were infusion-related immediate hypersensitivity reactions manifesting as pruritus, flushing, rash, and facial swelling. Clinical worsening of COVID-19 after monoclonal antibody administration may include but is not limited to fever, fatigue, hypoxia or increased respiratory difficulty, arrhythmias, and altered mental status.

retrieved_docs.title: Etesevimab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Etesevimab and bamlanivimab infusions should be given only by a qualified healthcare professional in an outpatient facility who can manage hypersensitive reactions. The patient should be monitored for anaphylaxis for at least 1 hour after infusion completion.

retrieved_docs.title: Emicizumab -- Administration -- Use in Specific Patient Population
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding Considerations: No data is available on the clinical use of emicizumab during breastfeeding. Emicizumab is a large protein molecule with high molecular weight; hence the concentration in milk is probably low. Hypothetically absorption of emicizumab is unlikely because it is probably destroyed in the infant's gastrointestinal tract. [18] According to product labeling, clinicians should consider the health benefits and risks of exposure to emicizumab in breastfeeding infants and the maternal need for emicizumab.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medications -- Clinical Significance -- Monoclonal Antibody Immune-Modulating Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Newer class monoclonal antibody immune-modulating drugs can be used in specific situations where patients do not respond effectively to inhaled glucocorticoid therapy and are found to have eosinophilia. These medications aim to decouple the Th2 inflammatory pathway to decrease the immune response to a triggering event, effectively decreasing eosinophilia.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Erenumab : The only contraindication for the use of erenumab is hypersensitivity to drugs or excipients. The current commercially available form of erenumab makes use of substances such as latex. Allergic history to such substances should also be obtained before administration. [13] Eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab: These drugs are contraindicated in patients with serious hypersensitivity to these or any of their excipients. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab) are used for migraine prophylaxis in episodic and chronic migraine. Rimegepant and ubrogepant are used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. galcanezumab and fremanezumab are under development for the preventive treatment of cluster headaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are identified as drugs used in hereditary angioedema, and both are classified as members of the therapeutic group “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema.” This drug class is itself a subclass of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) category “other hematological agents,” linking ecallantide and icatibant to a broader group of hematology‑related medications. Heme products, described as pharmacologic substances, are also placed under the same ATC category “other hematological agents,” indicating that they share a common therapeutic classification with the hereditary angioedema drugs. Crizanlizumab is described as a therapeutic agent belonging to the class of immunological agents. It is characterized as an immunomodulator and an immunosuppressive agent, and more specifically as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor. These disposition and product descriptors reinforce its role in modulating immune and inflammatory pathways. Consistent with the classification

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 171
retrieved_docs.content: Chemical (ATC) classification, crizanlizumab is a member of the category “other hematological agents in ATC.” This same ATC group also encompasses drugs used in hereditary angioedema—specifically ecallantide and icatibant—as well as heme products (pharmacologic substances) and voxelotor, indicating a broader therapeutic class of hematologic agents. The class “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema” is itself a subclass of “other hematological agents in ATC,” linking ecallantide and icatibant to the same ATC family as crizanlizumab, heme products, and voxelotor. This hierarchical organization highlights the shared hematologic focus of these agents, despite their diverse mechanisms such as complement inhibition (ecallantide), bradykinin‑B2 receptor antagonism (icatibant), selective adhesion‑molecule inhibition (crizanlizumab), and hemoglobin‑modifying activity (voxelotor). Overall, the relationships illustrate how various pharmacologic substances—ranging from metabolic agents like

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 541
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs used in hereditary angioedema comprise ecallantide and icatibant, both of which are classified within the broader group identified as E [Preparations]. The E [Preparations] class also encompasses other agents such as efinaconazole, which shares the same parent classification, and enoxaparin, which is a direct member of this preparation group. This organization shows that while ecallantide and icatibant are specifically indicated for hereditary angioedema, they share a common formulation or chemical class with efinaconazole and enoxaparin, highlighting the relevance of preparation‑based categorization for understanding pharmacologic properties, potential drug interactions, and cross‑reactivity in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor. These disposition and product descriptors reinforce its role in modulating immune and inflammatory pathways. Consistent with the classification of the hereditary angioedema drugs, crizanlizumab is also a member of the ATC group “other hematological agents,” demonstrating that diverse agents—ranging from anti‑angioedema peptides to targeted monoclonal antibodies—can be grouped together within this hematology‑focused category. Voxelotor, another pharmacologic substance, is likewise listed as a member of “other hematological agents,” further illustrating the breadth of therapies encompassed by this ATC class. Together, these relationships highlight how ecallantide, icatibant, crizanlizumab, heme products, and voxelotor are interconnected through shared ATC classification, while also possessing distinct mechanistic attributes such as inhibition of selective adhesion molecules or modulation of immune responses. Recognizing both the common

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 563
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are pharmacologic agents that belong to the therapeutic class of drugs used in hereditary angioedema. Both medications are specifically indicated for the management of hereditary angioedema attacks, targeting the underlying mechanisms of the disorder to reduce swelling and improve patient outcomes. Their inclusion in this drug class highlights their shared role in treating this rare, potentially life‑threatening condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 215
retrieved_docs.content: preparation label. The therapeutic area “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema” comprises ecallantide and icatibant, both biologic agents targeting bradykinin‑mediated angioedema. In the ATC system, aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen and nusinersen are grouped under “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” reflecting their application in musculo‑skeletal diseases. Nusinersen is the child of the “N [Preparations]” group and exerts a physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, which underlies its therapeutic action. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy that links individual drugs to ATC categories, preparation hierarchies, pharmacologic classes (including inverse‑ISA relationships), and specific therapeutic indications, thereby providing a comprehensive view of their roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 416
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as drugs for obstructive airway diseases. Within this broad category, the ATC hierarchy defines two specialized subgroups: “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” and “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants.” The subgroup “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” is an inverse‑isa of the parent class “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” indicating that it is a more specific category under the overall group. The same inverse‑isa relationship exists for the inhalant subgroup, and the inhalant subgroup is also explicitly linked to the parent class by an isa relationship, confirming its status as a direct child of the broader category. Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are members of the systemic subgroup, reflecting that they are administered systemically (e.g., oral, subcutaneous or intravenous routes) for the treatment of conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and other obstructive airway disorders. Fenspiride is listed as a member of both the systemic subgroup and the inhalant subgroup, suggesting that it may be used either systemically or as an inhaled preparation in the management of obstructive airway disease. The inclusion of biologic agents such as benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab highlights the role of targeted monoclonal‑antibody therapies in severe asthma and related airway inflammation, while amlexanox’s placement in the systemic subgroup points to its potential off‑label or investigational use for airway disease. This ATC classification framework clarifies the therapeutic positioning of each drug, aids clinicians in selecting appropriate treatment routes, and supports consistent pharmacovigilance and prescribing practices for patients with obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1072
retrieved_docs.content: The category E [Preparations] serves as a parent grouping for several pharmaceutical agents, including efedrin and enoxaparin, which are classified as child preparations within this group. Ecallantide is also associated with E [Preparations], reflecting its inclusion in this broader preparation category. In addition to its placement under E [Preparations], ecallantide is specifically identified as a member of the therapeutic class Drugs used in hereditary angioedema, a category that also comprises icatibant. This dual classification indicates that ecallantide functions both as a general preparation within E [Preparations] and as a targeted treatment for hereditary angioedema. Recognizing these hierarchical and categorical relationships helps clinicians understand the broader formulation context of efedrin, enoxaparin, and ecallantide while also appreciating the specific clinical indication of ecallantide and icatibant for hereditary angioedema.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2484
retrieved_docs.content: E [Preparations] is a broad category of pharmaceutical preparations that encompasses several distinct drugs, including efedrin, ecallantide, and enoxaparin. As the parent class, E [Preparations] groups these agents together, indicating that each of them—efedrin, a sympathomimetic agent; ecallantide, a kallikrein inhibitor used for hereditary angioedema; and enoxaparin, a low‑molecular‑weight heparin anticoagulant—belongs to the same overarching preparation class. This classification highlights their shared status as formulated medicinal products while recognizing their individual therapeutic roles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 215
retrieved_docs.content: are specific examples of the H1‑histamine antagonist class (inverse‑ISA of the class). Fenspiride belongs simultaneously to the inhalant and systemic drug groups, reflecting its dual formulation. The preparation group “E [Preparations]” serves as a parent category for several diverse agents: it lists ecallantide and efinaconazole as parent agents, and includes enoxaparin, eprozinol and eteplirsen as child preparations. Within this hierarchy, eprozinol (and similarly protopine) is defined as an inverse‑is‑a of the H1‑histamine antagonist class, indicating that it does not act as a conventional H1 antagonist. Enalapril (a cardiovascular drug) and efedrin (a sympathomimetic) are also members of the “E [Preparations]” group, illustrating how unrelated therapeutic agents can share a common preparation label. The therapeutic area “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema” comprises ecallantide and icatibant, both biologic agents targeting bradykinin‑mediated angioedema. In the ATC system,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 1088
retrieved_docs.content: E [Preparations] is a parent category that includes several distinct pharmaceutical agents. Enalapril, an angiotensin‑converting enzyme inhibitor, and efedrin, a sympathomimetic amine, are both listed as child preparations of E [Preparations]. Enoxaparin, a low‑molecular‑weight heparin used for anticoagulation, is also a child of this category. In addition, ecallantide and efinaconazole are described as having E [Preparations] as their parent, indicating that they belong to the same overarching preparation family. Ecallantide is further classified as a member of the therapeutic group “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema,” which also includes icatibant, another agent specifically indicated for hereditary angioedema. Thus, while enalapril, efedrin, enoxaparin, ecallantide, and efinaconazole share the common lineage of E [Preparations], ecallantide and icatibant are uniquely linked by their role in treating hereditary angioedema. This hierarchical organization clarifies how these drugs are related both by their broader preparation class and by their specific clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 909
retrieved_docs.content: E [Preparations] is presented as a hierarchical grouping that functions as the parent category for the drugs enalapril and eteplirsen, indicating that these two agents are specific preparations within the broader E [Preparations] class. At the same time, E [Preparations] is positioned beneath the larger parent entities ecallantide and efinaconazole, suggesting that it is a subclass derived from or related to these agents. In the therapeutic context, the collection “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema” includes ecallantide and icatibant as its members, linking both drugs to the treatment of hereditary angioedema. Consequently, ecallantide serves a dual role: it is a parent of E [Preparations] in the classification hierarchy and a member of the hereditary‑angioedema drug group, while icatibant is solely a member of that therapeutic group. This network of relationships illustrates how specific pharmaceutical preparations (enalapril, eteplirsen) are organized under broader categories (E [Preparations]), which in turn are nested within even larger parent classes (ecallantide, efinaconazole), and how certain agents (ecallantide, icatibant) are grouped together based on their clinical use in hereditary angioedema, providing a clear framework for understanding drug classification and therapeutic application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: Blood‑and‑blood‑forming organ drugs, hematological enzymes, heme‑derived pharmacologic substances, and treatments for hereditary angioedema are all classified in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other hematological agents,” making these terms interchangeable within this hierarchy. Within this group, the hemostatic effects subgroup aligns with vitamin K and other hemostatics, indicating that agents with vitamin K‑like activity belong to the same class. Specific members of the broader “agent affecting blood or body fluid” category include carglumic acid, dextrose monohydrate, and the monoclonal antibody crizanlizumab; crizanlizumab is further described as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor, an immunological agent, and both an immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive medication. The enzyme class “deoxyribonucleases / plasmin” contains the human fibrinolysin preparation and falls under the hematological enzymes subgroup, which together with deoxyribonukleasy (e.g., streptodornase) is placed in the same ATC category. Drugs used in hereditary angioedema, such as ecallantide and icatibant, are also members of “other hematological agents,” linking them to the same therapeutic family as the hematological enzymes and heme‑derived products. Voxelotor shares this ATC classification with crizanlizumab, underscoring that diverse agents—including hemostatics, enzyme preparations, immunomodulatory antibodies, metabolic sugars, and specific hereditary angioedema treatments—are unified under the “other hematological agents” umbrella, reflecting their common relevance to blood formation, coagulation, and immune‑mediated blood disorders and aiding clinicians in therapeutic selection and anticipation of class‑related effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 324
retrieved_docs.content: E [Preparations] is a classification of pharmaceutical preparations that includes efedrin as one of its constituent agents. Within this classification, ecallantide and efinaconazole are also linked, reflecting a hierarchical relationship in which E [Preparations] can be considered a subcategory of the broader entities represented by ecallantide and efinaconazole. Additionally, ecallantide, together with icatibant, belongs to the specific therapeutic group “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema,” indicating that these agents are employed in the management of hereditary angioedema. This network of relationships highlights how individual drugs such as efedrin, ecallantide, icatibant, and efinaconazole are organized within broader preparation categories and therapeutic classes, informing clinicians about their classification and potential clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1446
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, distinguishing them from inhaled bronchodilators and corticosteroids. By sharing this classification, these agents are recognized as systemic options that target specific inflammatory pathways in severe or refractory disease, helping clinicians select appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2792
retrieved_docs.content: Methapyrilen and omalizumab are both assigned to the therapeutic class of substances that affect the respiratory system. As members of this class, they are used to influence breathing function and are relevant in the treatment of respiratory disorders. Omalizumab, an anti‑IgE monoclonal antibody, is employed in the management of allergic asthma and other IgE‑mediated respiratory conditions, while methapyrilen, a respiratory‑acting agent, is utilized to modify airway dynamics. Recognizing that both drugs belong to the same therapeutic category helps clinicians consider them when selecting pharmacologic strategies aimed at modulating respiratory function.
